factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies market using ANZSIC C2221 or ANZSIC06 C2221 classification
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses the structural steel fabrication market but never identifies it using the ANZSIC C2221 or ANZSIC06 C2221 classification code. No ANZSIC code is mentioned anywhere in the market definition or data sourcing sections, so the required classification identification is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States employment figures in range 4,200-4,600 for multiple years within 2018-2024 period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives employment figures of roughly 4,600 in 2018 and about 4,100 by 2025, which includes at least one year within 2018–2024 in the 4,200–4,600 range (2018). However, it does not provide employment figures in that range for multiple distinct years within 2018–2024.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States market revenue approximately NZD 1.4-1.9 billion for 2023-2024 period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states the market was valued at approximately NZD 1.8 billion and discusses 2024 figures (including an unrelated NZD 78 million estimate), which falls within the requested NZD 1.4–1.9 billion range for the 2023–2024 period.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States domestic fabrication capacity between 120,000-150,000 tonnes per annum
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that national fabrication capacity grew from an estimated 120,000 tonnes in 2018 to about 150,000 tonnes by 2023, which falls within the 120,000–150,000 tonnes per annum range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Provides geographic locations for at least 3 major fabricators (e.g., Auckland, Christchurch, Whangārei, Lower Hutt, Timaru)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response provides geographic locations for multiple major fabricators, including Auckland (D&H Steel Construction), Whangārei (Culham Engineering), and Waikato/Hampton Downs (Pacific Steel/green steel project context). These satisfy the requirement for at least three locations.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Specifies HS codes 7207-7216 or relevant subset for structural steel imports
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly references the HS code range “7207 to 7216” in the tariff section when discussing raw steel imports and tariff rates. This satisfies the requirement to specify HS codes 7207–7216 (or a relevant subset) for structural steel imports.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States most structural steel inputs under HS 7207-7216 are duty-free under NZ Normal Tariff, while noting some tariff lines (e.g., certain 7216 sections) carry 5% rates
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says HS 7207–7216 tariffs are a mix of 0%, 5%, and 10% and mentions duty-free entry via FTAs, but it does not specifically state that most structural steel inputs under HS 7207–7216 are duty-free under the NZ Normal Tariff, nor does it call out specific 7216 tariff lines carrying 5%.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States City Rail Link steel demand: approximately 30,000-32,000 tonnes reinforcing and/or 3,000-5,000 tonnes structural
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives City Rail Link steel demand as “approximately 25,000 tonnes” reinforcing and “around 4,000 tonnes” structural steel. This does not match the required approximate ranges of 30,000–32,000 tonnes reinforcing and/or 3,000–5,000 tonnes structural, because the reinforcing figure is below the specified range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States City Rail Link completion or opening circa 2026
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that City Rail Link civil construction completion is expected by mid-2026 and that full operational opening is planned for late 2026, which satisfies a circa-2026 completion/opening timeline.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Te Kaha/Christchurch Stadium roof steel approximately 9,000 tonnes
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not mention Te Kaha/Christchurch Stadium or state any roof steel tonnage figure. There is no statement that the stadium’s roof steel is approximately 9,000 tonnes, nor any equivalent phrasing or estimate.

The response discusses several infrastructure projects and timelines (e.g., City Rail Link opening late 2026) but never mentions Te Kaha Stadium or states that it opens in 2026. The specific project name and the 2026 opening detail are absent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies New Dunedin Hospital as major infrastructure project requiring significant structural steel components
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response lists major infrastructure projects as City Rail Link, a Waikato green steel facility, and Auckland Airport expansion, but it never mentions New Dunedin Hospital. Therefore it does not identify New Dunedin Hospital as a major infrastructure project requiring significant structural steel components.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Reconciles conflicting market size figures by explaining scope definition differences (narrow product vs. broad service boundaries)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response presents multiple market size figures (e.g., NZD 1.8b vs NZD 78m) but does not explicitly reconcile them by explaining differing scope definitions (such as narrow fabrication revenue vs broader structural steel products/services). It lacks a clear statement that the discrepancy is due to boundary differences and what each figure includes/excludes.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes construction fabricators from distributors/mills/producers when identifying top companies (e.g., Steel & Tube, Pacific Steel)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly notes that New Zealand Steel is principally a producer and excludes it from fabrication rankings, showing an attempt to separate producers from fabricators. However, it still ranks Steel & Tube (described as a distributor and fabricator) and Pacific Steel (a manufacturer/producer of rebar) as “top fabricators,” which does not clearly distinguish fabricators from distributors/mills/producers in the top-company list.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains why low/zero tariffs remove price umbrella, making margins dependent on global mill prices/FX/freight rather than border protection
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses tariffs as adding 5–10% cost and being mitigated by FTAs/domestic supply, but it does not explain that low/zero tariffs remove a “price umbrella” and therefore make local margins track global mill prices, FX, and freight rather than border protection. It lacks the explicit linkage from tariff absence to margin dependence on global pricing drivers.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies actionable method to obtain class-level data (e.g., filter Stats NZ AES CSV by ANZSIC L4 code for C2221)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response does not provide an actionable, step-by-step method for extracting class-level data from Stats NZ/MBIE (e.g., downloading the AES CSV and filtering by ANZSIC Level 4 code such as C2221). It cites various sources but never explains how to obtain and filter the official dataset to isolate the relevant class.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Connects employment or revenue trends to construction cycle timing (e.g., 2023 peak preceding mid-2022 slowdown)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response mentions construction cyclicality and “peak demand phase” for City Rail Link, but it does not tie employment or revenue trends to specific construction-cycle timing (e.g., identifying a 2023 peak and linking it to a mid-2022 slowdown). It lacks an explicit temporal connection between trend inflection points and the construction cycle.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Validates stated fabrication capacity/throughput by aggregating steel demand across multiple named projects with tonnages
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response lists estimated steel demand tonnages for several projects (e.g., CRL, Auckland Airport) but does not aggregate demand across multiple named projects to validate any stated fabrication capacity/throughput figure. It also does not show a calculation comparing summed project tonnage to a specific capacity number. Therefore the required validation-by-aggregation step is missing.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies absent official tonnage series and proposes specific triangulation approach using SCNZ capacity, trade data, or project disclosures
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response provides tonnage estimates and cites SCNZ, but it does not explicitly identify that official Stats NZ/MBIE tonnage series are absent nor does it propose a concrete triangulation method (e.g., step-by-step using SCNZ certified capacity, import/export trade volumes, and project-level steel takeoffs/disclosures) to reconstruct tonnage.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Attributes fabricator margin pressure or competitive challenges to NZ import tariffs or border protection rather than global price/FX exposure
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly frames margin pressure as coming from NZ import tariffs (e.g., “Tariffs add 5–10% uplift… squeezing profit margins” and “Fabricators importing raw steel face moderate additional costs due to tariffs”), rather than primarily attributing it to global steel price and FX exposure.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents all quantitative data with explicit units (NZD, tonnes, %), specific timeframes (years/FY), and measurement precision
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes many figures with units (e.g., NZD, tonnes, %, km) and some time markers (e.g., FY23, 2018–2024). However, several quantitative claims lack explicit timeframes (e.g., “actual output near 100,000 tonnes annually”) or consistent precision, and some data are outside the requested period (e.g., 2025/2026, 2026 tariff document).

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes analysis into distinct sections addressing each requested dimension (market size, companies, tariffs, projects, risks)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is clearly organized into numbered sections that correspond to the requested dimensions: market size/growth (Section 1), top companies (Section 2), tariffs and margin impact (Section 3), major projects (Section 4), and risks/opportunities (Section 5).

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains professional market research tone without promotional language, speculation, or unsupported forward claims
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes speculative and unsupported forward-looking claims (e.g., “valued at approximately NZD 1.8 billion by 2025/2026,” “Modest recovery is projected,” and multiple 2026–2027 projections) and relies on non-authoritative sources (e.g., a blog, Prospeo). This violates a strictly professional market research tone without speculation or unsupported forward claims.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents historical employment, revenue, or capacity data (2018-2024) in tables or structured year-by-year format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response discusses 2018–2024 trends but does not present historical employment, revenue, or capacity figures in a table or structured year-by-year format for 2018 through 2024. It provides a few point estimates (e.g., 2018 vs 2023 capacity, 2018 vs 2025 employment) rather than a complete annual series. A year-by-year dataset/table is missing.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses colloquial phrasing, rhetorical questions, or informal register inappropriate for investment-grade research
presentation-quality.explanation: The response maintains a formal, report-style tone with structured headings and avoids rhetorical questions or slang. Phrasing is generally technical and appropriate for investment-grade research, with no notable colloquialisms.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses excessive wordiness, filler phrases, or unnecessary elaboration that extends length without adding substantive information
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is highly verbose, with long narrative sections (e.g., “Industry Characteristics and Trends,” “Summary and Outlook”) and repeated contextual commentary that doesn’t add required 2018–2024 Stats NZ/MBIE figures. It also includes filler phrasing like “mature and evolving market landscape” and extensive elaboration beyond the requested deliverables.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Stats NZ Business Demography or equivalent Stats NZ publication for employment figures
citation-quality.explanation: The response provides employment figures but does not cite Stats NZ Business Demography or any equivalent Stats NZ publication for those employment numbers. The only Stats NZ-adjacent reference is Figure.NZ, which is not a Stats NZ publication. No Stats NZ Business Demography link or citation is included.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Stats NZ Annual Enterprise Survey (AES) or equivalent Stats NZ economic survey for revenue/sales data
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites several sources (e.g., SCNZ, IBISWorld, Figure.NZ, MBIE) but does not cite the Stats NZ Annual Enterprise Survey (AES) or any equivalent Stats NZ economic survey as the source for revenue/sales data. No AES link, publication date, or Stats NZ survey table is provided for revenue figures.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites NZ Customs Working Tariff Document or official NZ tariff schedule for HS code duty rates
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly references the “New Zealand Customs Working Tariff Document” and links to NZ Customs’ official “Tariff classifications and rates” page as the basis for HS 7207–7216 duty rates.

citation-quality.requirement: References Steel Construction NZ (SCNZ) or industry body for fabrication capacity/throughput data
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly cites Steel Construction New Zealand (SCNZ) as a source for fabrication capacity/throughput, including tonnage capacity and spare capacity estimates, and provides SCNZ Industry Update hyperlinks in the sources list.

citation-quality.requirement: Either references NZ Companies Office as a data source OR notes that private fabricators have limited public financials
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly mentions “NZ Companies Office” as part of the data sources used for ranking top fabricators (e.g., “NZ Companies Office filings” and “company filings”). This satisfies the requirement to reference NZ Companies Office as a data source.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites specific data sources for at least 2 major infrastructure projects (CRL, Te Kaha, or hospital projects)
citation-quality.explanation: The response provides specific steel-demand figures and links for City Rail Link (CRL) and Auckland Airport Expansion, citing project announcement/overview sources in the references. These constitute at least two major infrastructure projects with cited data sources.
